stem: Find the area of the region enclosed between the parabola \( x = y^{2} \) and the line \( x = [[m]]y + ([[k]]) \), integrating with respect to \( y \) using horizontal strips.
solution: The curves meet where \( y^{2} = [[m]]y + ([[k]]) \), namely at \( y = [[p]] \) and \( y = [[q]] \). A horizontal strip has width \( \text{right} - \text{left} = [[m]]y + ([[k]]) - y^{2} \), so the area is \( \int_{[[p]]}^{[[q]]}\left([[m]]y + ([[k]]) - y^{2}\right)dy = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This doubles the correct area.
This triples the correct area.
This is the square of the vertical extent, not the area.
This integrates \( \text{left} - \text{right} \), giving the negative of the area.
This is off by one from the correct area.

stem: The sideways curve \( x = y(y-[[p]])(y-[[q]]) \) crosses the \( y \)-axis at \( y = 0, [[p]], [[q]] \), lying to the right of the axis on part of \( 0 \le y \le [[q]] \) and to the left on the rest. Using horizontal strips, find the TOTAL area of the region(s) enclosed between the curve and the \( y \)-axis on this range.
solution: The curve is to the right of the \( y \)-axis on \( (0,[[p]]) \) and to the left on \( ([[p]],[[q]]) \), so the total (unsigned) area requires \( \int_{0}^{[[q]]}\left|y(y-[[p]])(y-[[q]])\right|dy = [[answer]] \), not the plain (signed) integral.
distractor_rationales: This is the plain signed integral, which cancels the two regions instead of adding their areas.
This only counts one of the two enclosed regions.
This double-counts the enclosed area.
This is off by \( [[p]] \) from the correct total area.
This is off by \( [[p]] \) from the correct total area.
This treats the region as a triangle-like shape with no calculus basis.

stem: Find the area of the region enclosed between the rightward-opening curve \( x = y^{2} + ([[p]]) \) and the leftward-opening curve \( x = -y^{2} + [[q]] \), using horizontal strips.
solution: The curves meet where \( y^{2}+([[p]]) = -y^{2}+[[q]] \), i.e. at \( y = \pm[[s]] \). The leftward curve lies to the right of the rightward one between these points, so the area is \( \int_{-[[s]]}^{[[s]]}\left((-y^{2}+[[q]]) - (y^{2}+([[p]]))\right)dy = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This doubles the correct area.
This halves the correct area.
This is the square of the vertical extent, not the area.
This integrates \( \text{left} - \text{right} \), giving the negative of the area.
This is off by one from the correct area.

stem: Find the area of the triangular region enclosed between the sideways V-shaped curve \( x = |y - [[h]]| \) and the vertical line \( x = [[k]] \), using horizontal strips.
solution: The curves meet where \( |y-[[h]]| = [[k]] \), i.e. \( y = [[h]]-[[k]] \) and \( y = [[h]]+[[k]] \). The line lies to the right of the V-shape between these points, giving a triangle of base \( 2[[k]] \) and height \( [[k]] \): \( \int_{[[h]]-[[k]]}^{[[h]]+[[k]]}\left([[k]] - |y-[[h]]|\right)dy = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the full base times height, \( 2k \cdot k \), instead of half that for a triangle.
This computes half the triangle's area as if the base were only \( k \).
This doubles the correct area.
This integrates \( \text{left} - \text{right} \), giving the negative of the area.
This is off by \( [[h]] \) from the correct area.

stem: On the interval \( [[a]] \le y \le [[b]] \), the curve \( x = y^{2} + [[p]] \) lies entirely to the right of the line \( x = [[q]]y \). Using horizontal strips, find the area of the region between them on this interval.
solution: Since \( y^{2}+[[p]]-[[q]]y \) has no real roots (its discriminant \( [[q]]^{2}-4[[p]] \) is negative), the curve stays to the right of the line on the whole given interval, and the area is directly \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]}\left((y^{2}+[[p]]) - [[q]]y\right)dy = [[answer]] \), with no need to solve for intersection points first.
distractor_rationales: This evaluates the horizontal gap only at \( y=[[b]] \) minus at \( y=[[a]] \), instead of integrating it.
This uses the gap at \( y=[[b]] \) times the width, a right-rectangle approximation.
This integrates \( \text{left} - \text{right} \), giving the negative of the area.
This doubles the correct area.
This is off by \( [[p]] \) from the correct area.
